questions.q: What is quantization in the context of analog-to-digital conversion?
options: The process of measuring the signal's frequency content
Rounding analog sample values to the nearest discrete digital level
Filtering out frequencies above the Nyquist rate
The rate at which samples are collected from an analog signal
questions.explanation: Quantization maps infinite analog values to a finite set of discrete digital values. An ADC with b bits produces 2^b quantization levels. This introduces quantization error, typically modeled as uniform random noise.

questions.q: An ADC has 8 bits and an input range of 0 to 10V. What is the quantization step size?
options: 8 V
questions.explanation: Quantization step (least significant bit, LSB) = (Vmax - Vmin) / 2^b = 10 / 256 ≈ 0.039 V. With 256 levels, each adjacent level differs by this amount. Maximum quantization error is ± LSB/2.

questions.q: What is the Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) for an ideal ADC, assuming full-scale sinusoidal input?
options: SNR = 2^b (where b is bit depth)
SNR ≈ 6.02·b + 1.76 dB (logarithmic)
SNR depends only on the sampling rate
SNR is always constant at 20 dB
questions.explanation: For an ideal b-bit ADC, SQNR ≈ 6.02·b + 1.76 dB. A 16-bit ADC yields ≈98 dB SNR, an 8-bit yields ≈50 dB. This assumes uniform quantization and full-scale signal; smaller signals have worse SNR due to fewer effective bits.

questions.q: What does a Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC) do?
options: Converts analog signals to digital bit patterns for storage
Converts digital binary values back into an analog signal by summing weighted current or voltage sources
Removes high frequencies to anti-alias the signal
Increases the resolution of a digital signal
questions.explanation: A DAC reconstructs an analog signal from digital samples, typically using a weighted summing network (binary-weighted resistors or capacitor networks). The output is then smoothed with a reconstruction filter.

questions.q: What is the relationship between sampling rate and the maximum frequency that can be represented in a digital signal?
options: The maximum frequency is the sampling rate minus 1
The maximum frequency is half the sampling rate (the Nyquist frequency)
There is no relationship; any frequency can be represented at any sampling rate
The maximum frequency is twice the sampling rate
questions.explanation: By the Nyquist theorem, the highest unambiguous frequency in a sampled signal is fs/2. Frequencies above this alias into the baseband. This fundamental limit applies regardless of ADC resolution or design.

questions.q: What causes quantization distortion, and how does it appear in the frequency domain?
options: Sampling at rates below Nyquist; it appears as aliasing
Rounding sample values to discrete levels; it introduces harmonic distortion and broadband noise floor
High-order filters; it shifts the phase of all frequency components equally
Insufficient anti-aliasing filtering; it produces only DC bias
questions.explanation: Quantization error is typically modeled as white noise uniformly distributed ±LSB/2. For large signals, this manifests as broadband noise; for small signals, deterministic harmonic distortion appears. Dithering converts it to perceptually softer noise.

questions.q: What is 'bit depth' or 'resolution' in a digital signal?
options: The number of samples collected per second
The number of bits used to represent each sample, determining the number of quantization levels
The frequency range the ADC can measure
How deeply the ADC can amplify weak signals
questions.explanation: Bit depth (b) directly determines quantization levels: 2^b possible values. Higher bit depth reduces quantization error and increases SNR, at the cost of more data per sample and ADC complexity.

questions.q: Why is the reconstruction filter (analog low-pass filter after a DAC) necessary?
options: To remove the DC component of the signal
To eliminate imaging—copies of the spectrum at multiples of the sampling frequency introduced by DAC reconstruction
To increase the amplitude of the reconstructed signal
To convert the digital values back to analog (the DAC itself cannot do this)
questions.explanation: A DAC outputs analog step approximations; without filtering, these steps contain the original signal plus copies (images) centered at fs, 2fs, etc. The reconstruction filter removes these images, leaving only the baseband signal 0 to fs/2.

questions.q: What is 'effective number of bits' (ENOB) in an ADC?
options: The same as the nominal bit depth specified by the manufacturer
The number of bits equivalent to the actual SNR achieved, accounting for nonlinearity and noise
The number of bits required to store the ADC output in memory
The number of bits used only for the most significant part of the signal
questions.explanation: ENOB = (SNRdB - 1.76) / 6.02, giving the equivalent bit depth if only quantization noise were present. Real ADCs have ENOB less than nominal bits due to noise, distortion, and nonlinearity from the analog circuitry.

questions.q: A signal is acquired by a 12-bit ADC with range 0-5V. If the true analog value is 2.537V, what is the likely digital output range?
options: Exactly 2537 counts (precise digital mapping)
A range due to quantization error; approximately 1550-1555 counts out of 4096 possible
Only 2048 (exactly half the range)
A value that requires compensation by a DAC to recover accurately
questions.explanation: 12 bits give 4096 levels across 5V (LSB ≈ 1.22 mV). The value 2.537V maps to count ≈ 2537/5 × 4096 ≈ 2076. Quantization error causes variation of ±1 count (±0.61 mV). The exact output depends on the rounding method (round, truncate, etc.).
